It's almost time for Matins. I should go.
Goodbye, Senior Federico.
Federico Giallo stands alone in the morning light of the sacrarium.
The marks left behind by the flames have already been scrubbed away. Deprived of its furnishings, the sacrarium feels empty, with only the sound of footsteps to fill it.
Federico is an outstanding Executor. He has been with the Notarial Hall for six years, carrying out his duties with excellence.
But this is his first year as a saint. There is no one to guide him or instruct him.
The silent saint picks up the broken and burned flower.
The statue protected it from the flames. It was a miracle that it survived the fire.
But this miracle is worthless in the eyes of another.
The flower is still here.
But it is not the one he wanted?
Wounds beyond repair...
I don't understand.
Would Laterano still be Laterano?
Insufficient data to reach a conclusion.
Executor Federico realizes with a start—
Clément's expression was too calm.
He did not mention Gerald's death, nor the arrangements of the other Sarkaz.
What about himself?
Words without action have no power. That is why he did not say he would find out the truth.
Nor did he say he would fulfill Gerald's last request.
Federico gazes at the broken flower before his eyes.
He has a feeling—
A feeling that, someday, he will find the answer to all his questions in this flower.
Mm—uh—
Morning, Sara... it's time to wake up...
Mm... mmm... zzz...
Eren! Wake up, Eren!
Mamma came back yesterday!
Huh?!
She came back? Where is she?
It must be her! Look—
A new blanket from Mamma? It's a little torn...
But there are pictures of circles on it! And people with wings! Like what Mamma promised us!
Who cares if it's a little torn? Mamma's clothes are torn too. We have to be good.
I know, I know.
Let's go and find Senior Federico!
Can we play with Senior Federico today?
No, silly, we're going to tell Senior Federico that Mamma came back to see us last night, so he doesn't need to worry!
There you are, Father.
It's your turn to clean the chapel today, Jori?
Gratias.
Just doing my job. I had trouble sleeping anyway.
I'll fetch the sacramental bread.
No, no need.
Oh, right, look at me, dust all over my hands.
Say, do you smell something...? It's pretty faint...
A strange smell?
No, a pleasant one. It's fresh. I sense in it... vitality.
It's hard to put into words. It's like standard downwind. I don't know where it comes from, but I feel like I'd get somewhere if I followed it.
I think it's the smell of flowers, Jori.
We don't have enough flour, so flower powder was added to the sacramental bread.
The few remaining bottles of wine in the cellar have started to go sour, so I also added some flower powder to those too.
Clément harvested some flowers a couple of years ago, grinding them into powder and storing it in the cellar...
He loves the flowers, just as he loves us.
Mm...
This will be the final communion we take before the abbey departs. We are short on food, however, so we must reduce each share of sacramental bread, even mixing in... other grains.
Please don't say that. I'm very much looking forward to today's communion...
Then go and wash your face. Come back with the others when it's time for Matins.
I'll do the rest myself.
Yes, Father.
Will we be saved after today, Father?
All of us, Sankta and Sarkaz... will we all be saved?
Even though Gerald is already...
Go, Jori.
O-Okay, see you later, Father.
The old man closes the door.
He climbs the chancel slowly.
Stefano Torregrossa, the aging abbot of Sanctilaminium Ambrosii, carefully divides the sacramental bread and wine.
His head is bowed. It is still early. The dim morning light shines through the windows behind the holy statue. The face of the statue is hidden in darkness, as is the abbot's own.
Yes. We will all be saved.
She walks into the broken sacrarium, crushing the charred remains of plants to powder beneath her feet.
The arched roof of the sacrarium has collapsed in the fire, exposing the interior in the sunlight.
The wind has carried away the smell, but there is no one to clean up the rubble.
The marks of the fire will remain forever, unless the chapel is rebuilt.
She takes a look at the collapsed statue, then abruptly turns to the empty space behind her.
Humans struggle, punish and forgive themselves, like a sonata at its climax, the keys dancing a frantic tune.
When a decision is made, the long and steady coda begins, the emptiness at the end of catharsis, yet infinitely determined.
Emotions are complex, but the changes are always alike. The melody is conventional, regular, devoid of surprise.
I sense your serenity. You have made your choice.
In any case, the song I should play is you. Hear the voice of your own heart, here at the very end.
You lost, vibrant soul.
All the pots and chopping boards... signs of baking, but no smell of yeast in the air... oh, I guess it's unleavened bread.
This looks like they were preparing the Eucharist here.
There is unidentified debris... it feels wet. Is it because we're underground?
No.
There is flour left in the corner... why is it so damp and sticky?
This smell...
Is this the only path the pious abbot saw, at the end of it all?
Can't get in touch with Federico... tell Oren? That will only make things more complicated.
Am I the one who's going to have to do this? I barely know where Iberia even is.
This is such a headache.
My forces will soon surround the monastery...
I will command them to block every path in and out. You stay here and coordinate with the assault.
Mm...
What is it?
Are you sure you want to do this, Oren?
Did you go over to Lemuen's side the moment you saw her? I don't think I need to waste my time explaining this again.
But I'll stop you, Oren.
Lemuen?
Ugh. She caught up.
Buy time while I rendezvous with the troops.
No way. Do you see the expression on her face?
I'm not getting on her bad side. I still remember when she dragged me out of school and confiscated my drone. I never saw it again.
Gratias, Serpilia.
You never thought to convince the abbot to bring the monastery back to Laterano.
That's your job.
You never intended to let any of the Sarkaz leave, you've even got commandos deployed to carry out your plan.
Fifty men... the limit of what I can muster.
What are the Lateran laws to you, Oren?
I will submit myself to His Holiness's judgment when we get back... though I don't think I've done anything that should be punished.
You know the consequences of an assault on the abbey.
The people here, both Sankta and Sarkaz, are like travelers who lost their way in the wilderness, who found warmth by a campfire.
The campfire guides their kin to rescue them, and other poor wandering souls.
They have no one to count on. They are weak and frightened. But they still hold on to their precious kindness.
That's why Gerald gave his life to plead with us, and ordered the Sarkaz under his leadership to keep the peace between the two sides.
But what if the campfire has attracted some beasts?
I wish I could say pretty words and do the kind of work everyone admires, Cardinal Aide Lemuen.
But I can't. You've never traveled the war-torn wastes with thousands upon thousands of refugees, seen the dark cloud that blankets the skies over Londinium.
But I thought you would at least have a tacit understanding with me, give some thought to the danger in letting those Sarkaz go.
The atrocities the Sarkaz committed in Victoria are well-known. They continue to spread their terror across the lands.
If you truly believe in His Holiness's plan, believe in the value of the Legati, then you should understand why Laterano can't be merciful here.
The friendship that Laterano built at the Summit of Nations is more fragile than the paper windows of this corridor.
The Sarkaz in the abbey are resentful, and affected by Arturia's Arts. There's no telling what lengths they will go to in order to get revenge after they leave.
Plus, what would happen if word got out that a Lateran nomadic abbey has been colluding with the Sarkaz for decades?
How will Laterano uphold its sanctity among the nations of Terra? How will she maintain her already-fragile diplomatic ties?
What will the Holy City look like when the fires of war burn?
Fifty men. That's all will take to resolve the situation here, and bury it all in darkness.
These Sarkaz are hunters, farmers... the only mercenary is dead. We saw proof with our own eyes.
You're planning a massacre.
You talk about upholding Laterano's sanctity, when you're the one defiling it. Don't you see the hypocrisy in that?
You're the hypocrite here, Lemuen.
The 'Lemuen the Silent' I know wouldn't have blinked at something like this.
Did five years asleep change you?
Or was it the so-called 'Martyr'—
Oren never managed to say the name.

The wheelchair closes the distance in the blink of an eye, and his midsection meets the barrel of a sniper rifle. Leaning against the wall, he barely manages to stay on his feet, and it takes all his effort to keep his voice level.
Gah—
Go on.
...Lemuen the Silent.
I told you not to get on her bad side.
Team Two at target location. Charges set.
All three exits on the blueprints of Sanctilaminium Ambrosii are under control.
If anyone tries to get out, the explosion will collapse the passage and force them back...
More like bury them under the rubble. Team Two was laughing about not being too precise here.
Go ahead.
Teams Three and Four have taken the high ground. We have sniper cover in all directions.
All teams in position.
Right on time.
Reverend Oren, we are in position to begin the assault on the Sarkaz in the abbey.
Repeat: We are in position.
What is it?
No response.
...That's not good.
Ha, don't worry about it. He probably has his hands full with an extra-large crepe.
Does that happen a lot?
All the time. First time on a special operation? Chill. The Lateran watches over you.
Just make sure you're ready. We'll go in the front door if we can't reach Oren, or if there's any unusual activity.
No one in the hallway. Quick, this way.
That way leads out the abbey. Aren't we looking for Gerald?
No, I need to get you to safety first.
Didn't you hear? The Laterans are taking the abbey by force. That woman, Serpilia, might have played nice, but the army won't.
You can't be seen by the Lateran army. You're fallen. To them, you're evi...
Go ahead, Raimund. I can handle it.
Don't... don't let me interrupt you, I did Fina.
All I meant to say is that you've never changed, not to me, Fortuna.
Anyway, you're in the same boat we are. It's only going to mean more trouble for the others if you stay behind.
...Alright.
I'll do as you say.
But what about you, Raimund? Will you be alright?
Don't worry, Gerald made plans for everything. I'm sure he anticipated something like this.
I'll come back and help him once I've found a safe place for you.
Your palms are sweating, Raimund. What's going on?
Nothing, just... a feeling I can't describe, like a heavy weight...
Maybe it's just that I didn't eat breakfast.
If not for all this, we would be having communion right now, with Gerald and everyone else...
We'll have plenty of chances.
Take a deep breath, Raimund. I'll be the one pulling you along now.
This way. The rooms here are all empty. No one will see us.
I'm fine. We're almost to the front door.
The Laterans might hear us opening the door. We go right as soon as we're outside. The rocks and tall grass should provide cover.
Are you ready? I pulled the latch.
Th-This is...
So that's why I had a feeling. Lateran troops.
Stay behind me, Fortuna.
...Team One here. Sarkaz sighted at the front door.
Good morning, Your Excellency.
Good morning again, Jori. Please, see to the others.
Yes, Father.
Father Abbot, we...
Much has happened of late, Sural. I understand your unease.
Should we have come? Would those people from Laterano...
It'll be alright.
All who dwell in the Sanctilaminium Ambrosii are welcome in this chapel. That's how things have been for the last ten years.
Sit up front, Sural. Just like always. No need to worry.
Laterans do not interrupt a communion in progress.
Please, take your seats.
Join me in a moment of prayer. Wait, listen, and feel the peace and tranquility.
I am Stefano Torregrossa, abbot of the Sanctilaminium Ambrosii.
The words 'believer' and 'kin' are not used here.
The mundane world has certain conceptions of the Sankta and the Sarkaz. Order and chaos. Peace and war. Angels and devils. We share the same conceptions.
Our halos and horns are our most distinctive features, symbols etched in the very essence of our life. Our existence alone draws the attention of others.
Yet we have seen the sun rise and set thousands of times together, and we gather here today to partake in communion, to share shelter and blessing.
We have spent varying lengths of time here at the abbey, but even the newcomers amongst us have been here for more than a decade.

For a much longer time, we had no way of knowing whether we had been abandoned or forgotten. We did not know what perils the path ahead would hold, that we would meet at a fork of which no one could know.
We had conflicts. We had secrets. But we tried to accept one another.
We know the names and habits of everyone in this chapel. We shared food and sheltered one another from wind and rain.
We fought beasts and bandits together. We built houses and paved roads together. We plowed fields and harvested them together.
We were brothers and sisters. We loved each other, and treasured one another's peace and happiness as our own.
The bonds that tie us went beyond any physical trait or telepathic connection, transcended any differences in faith or ideology.
Yet, after having endured the long night, it was the fleeting cold before the dawn that overcame us.
The nuncios from Laterano, the Executors, and... emissaries from a distant realm... they all had their own plans, brought new hope, and forced painful choices.
We lost our battered pillars to fire, and our respected and trusted brothers to conflict...
Stefano Torregrossa sought that which was most good, even if the answer was an unproven one. For this, I seek your forgiveness.
Sanctilaminium Ambrosii is not Paradise. But it is our common home.
I have faith that we will make the same choice.
Now, I invite you to take communion.
Does it hurt?
Could be worse.
You put on a brave face, but I can hear your teeth chattering.
The barrel of a rifle will never be worse than a bullet.
You can have one of those, too, if you want.
I can feel your seething anger.
You're angry that I violated the purity of your faith. That proves you're thinking as a Lateran.
Your mission is to bring the Sanctilaminium Ambrosii back to Laterano, and to help the lost Lateran kin there. My mission is to preserve what makes Laterano, Laterano.
Set your sentimentality aside, Lemuen. We're in agreement here.
Enough with your sophistry.
Did you learn it from the Victorian nobles, or Columbian merchants, Legatus Oren?
I said it, the sudden appearance of beasts will frighten wanderers. I'm not going to stand by and watch a massacre happen.
The commandos will be in position any moment now. You can't stop me.
I can incapacitate you, then deal with them.
You may have surprised me once, but it won't work again. Not unless you actually put a bullet in my brain.
Is that so?
Lemuen? Oren? Don't tell me you're really going to—
A loud crack interrupts the exchange.

Again, and then again, before anyone could react, so loud that it sounds like the patron firearm was being fired right next to their ears. They could not even hear their own heartbeats for a few seconds.
The projectile leaves a long trail in the damp morning air.
I am Executor Federico of the Laterano Notarial Hall. That was a warning shot.
Cardinal Aide Lemuen, Legatus Oren, and Technician Serpilia of the Sixth Tribunal. I command you to cease your pointless altercation at once.
I see no need to understand your positions or mediate your conflict.
The mission remains incomplete. Newly emerged factors continue to present a risk of escalation. This monastery needs order.
If there is no contradiction between you, yet conflict remains unresolved; if empathy is not enough to prevent a community from disintegrating...
Then I will be order.
That warning shot was also intended for the special forces outside. Lateran troops will not enter the Sanctilaminium Ambrosii without my express authorization.
You were the one who deployed them, Oren. You will restrain them. Order them to pull back one kilometer and stand by.
Lemuen and Serpilia. The two of you have been in contact with the Sankta and Sarkaz populations here. You will reassure them and prevent the situation from escalating.
Meet me in the chapel once you have finished your task. Richele has uncovered a lead and is following it.
I will find the one responsible for this situation.
You will act now.
Now, I invite you to take communion.
Guess I didn't make it in time.
Richele peeks through a gap in the door and sees the gathered crowd return to their seats with sacramental bread and wine.
In the chancel, the aging abbot looks up toward the door, almost as though he is looking at Richele.
Richele thinks for a moment, then closes the door. He thinks for another moment, then takes out a spare gun grip, which he uses to latch the door handle.
Almost. All that's left is to wait for it to... finish.
Hm? Did Federico run into trouble? I guess I'm the only one taking care of things here now.
What a chore.
I should have taken the mission to search for the missing Legatus in Sargon. It might have been a little far, but dealing with the people of the sand has got to be less complicated.
Am I doomed to be a workaholic? Truly, our fate lies not in our hands.
Let's see... patron firearm in good working order.
If everyone inside... is one bullet per head enough? Don't tell me I'll have to use my fists...
I should apply for paid leave once I get back to Laterano. The Notarial Hall had better approve it, considering my workload.
All is prepared. Come Matins, the people will take... take their final communion.
Will your strings... lament for me? Or will they celebrate me?
I am clueless about music, Arturia. Just as I cannot understand you.
Here you stand, and here I empathize with you, yet you seem a total blank.
I am no more than free to perform according to Your Excellency's will. My music is but a mirror.
A mirror... hah.
The elderly abbot lifts a porcelain tray, his hands quivering amidst string's overtones.
He appears as if wrestling with the sound that echoes throughout the dark, awaiting their end.
He was still young when the sounds of paradise arose in the abbey years and years ago, so young that his reflection in the fountain did not yet show a single hair of white.

Wild fowls were drawn to these roving soils, fluttering through skies rich with organ chords, flying for the square. In his old age, the last abbot handed the monastery down to him, Cleric Stefano, the youngest of them all.

lines.st: 'You must be their keeper, that they live in Paradise.'
And how? How would I see them stay in Paradise?

lines.x: Abbot Torregrossa shuts his eyes. Storied is his life.
The strings continue to echo, echo, echo. And then—
He lets his hands go.
How painful. So this is your choice.
From behind the sealed chapel entrance comes the muffled sound of speech.
Federico stands in the spacious hall for a while, listening, then turns to walk upstairs.
...Equipment normal.
The only loss has been one communicator.
Current objective remains unchanged. Proceeding with mission.
Locked onto suspect.
How're things in there?
Communion should be over by now.
Is it as bad in there as I think?
Signs point to yes.
Damn. How's Federico?
Unreachable.
The Church of the Deep doesn't breed pushovers for monsters. Does he know that?
Someone needs to do the work. Status on Lemuen and Serpilia?
They got your message and went underground to investigate.
Alright. That means the trickiest job of all is all ours.
Let's open up.
And be where we may, and face what we may, it remains that we must respect each other in kind, care for each other in kind—
—Good morning, emissaries of the Holy City.
The trays are empty.
Richele, get every inhabitant under control. I'll handle the abbot.
Is there something amiss, emissary?
Amiss? Well, here's the thing... what's got you so down, Most Reverend Abbot? So insistent that Matins today has to be the 'final' one?
I'm pretty sure Lemuen's informed you that after you dock back up with Laterano, there's a real chance you'll continue serving as this monastery's abbot...
Emissaries of Laterano, you question me... but on what basis?
I have made my choice—renounced my vain hopes that all could be met with equal salvation.
The monastery's course is run. On this final Matins, may all our trays be empty.
I have just delivered them the news. The Sanctilaminium Ambrosii has agreed to return to Laterano.
Return to Laterano...?
Sir, don't tell me you thought we'd let the Church of the Deep off the hook...
...I told you, my choice is made.
Oren, he's telling the truth.
The inhabitants haven't shown any signs of change. I've examined the trays, and they're clean.
Yes. I prepared the flesh of those monsters by my own hand, and mixed it all into the bread.
But though it was meant for Matins, I locked it away in a cabinet underground, lest my starving people ingest it accidentally.
Now that Aulus has left, that which you fear flourishes no longer.
This is the key to the cabinet door. Take it, it is my plea. This will prove that what I say is true. Perhaps you will know better how handle the Church of the Deep's givings.
Thank you for cooperating.
Looks like my gun's resting easy today...
What, are you sad about that?
Come again?
No, I'm just breathing out, finally. We got the rough part over with. Work should be getting back on track now... right?
Lemuen, catch what the abbot said? I'm bringing the key down now.
You don't have to.
Someone smashed the cabinet open. It's empty inside.
Yellowing napkins, pottery shards all over the floor. All the details line up with the abbot's description.
And looking at previous accounts, the consequences of eating this stuff are irreversible... If he'd made them partake already, he wouldn't need to lie to us anymore.
Which means someone's gone and thieved this little 'Eucharist.'
We have to find them immediately.
Uh, finding one person in an abbey this big—maybe a tall order?
It's hitting me. I think we've all forgotten a few things.
For one, Gerald heard about how that Sankta girl fell, and that dealt him a huge shock.
But we would've never let any word out about the falling. How did Gerald learn of it?
And for another, just what was the deal with that fire in the sacrarium?
Without someone else's encouragement, the abbot might not have been so dogmatic either...
After all of this, I've got a bad feeling our abbot's not alone in his despair.
When Fortuna went into the chapel, I remember somebody else was in there.

lines.st: So this is the Abbot's choice...
Not that it hasn't crossed my mind before—that things would ultimately turn out this way.
He is kindhearted. So kindhearted that he strove to this day, embracing such lofty ideals, and in turn, he lost to weakness at the very last moment.
But I am not like him.
It was long, long ago that I gave up hope.

lines.x: Oh? A guest.
I have to say, I'm a little surprised. I gave you fair warning, but...
I thought with all your rational, logical judgment, you'd conclude that 'there are other matters of higher priority to handle,' or something like that.
How long has it been since we last met, Federico? Five, maybe close to six years?
Hm? Aren't you going to say—'Discontinue this meaningless speech, fugitive Arturia?'
Or do you finally plan to switch to a different greeting?
Why not go back to those days when you lived with me, then... and call me Soror?
Oh...? And you're still not angry?
The first instant you came through the door and saw me, you didn't even make a move. That's unlike you.
Let me take a guess at why you're so out of sorts, then.
Are you waiting for something?
Or... are you hesitating?
Listen. Sounds of all kinds fill this little monastery.
Sorrow, pain, suspicion, resentment... and despair.
All woven together. More exquisitely touching than any man-made melody could ever be.
Ah... how very curious. Federico Giallo, have you felt anything? Is there any note out there that can dance within your brain, the one so different to how normal people think?
Fugitive Arturia, I have fully assessed your words and actions.
My present priority to address is not you.
You aren't in the chapel, Senior Federico?
I thought you and your colleagues all had your eyes fixed on the Most Reverend Abbot...
You are the prevailing suspect in this series of abnormalities.
I honor... yes, I honor His Excellency's choice.
We all respect him. Love him. This was always the way it would be, for we are all under His Excellency's wing...
It's simple to understand, isn't it?
When every cause sees you expelled from the cities, and your only choice is to roam the wastes, and you've become sure your end lies in death...
When your stomach groans with hunger, and all you ever find is despair, no matter where you walk, and eternity seems to have you trapped in the land that eats of man...
That's when past a gentle hill, and over the rock, you enter a canyon.
You see a building past the dust, a building that could dare to be a city, where its people bring you food, and let you stay in a room for the night.
Yes, there is only meager grit-like bread, and a bowl of watery soup.
Yes, there are cracks in the walls of the rooms, forever in need of repair...
But you have not died in the wastes. You have been admitted.
And that is why some of you call this place Paradise.
'Paradise'... yet it was a sham.
Not long after I arrived here, I found... that a Sarkaz friend of mine who'd caught Oripathy had vanished from my side.
I spent an age searching for her, only to find... she was here all along, in a room in the basement. They say it used to be a training room. Its walls are sturdier than all...
It's to that room where they send the deathly ill.
Improvised protective equipment cannot obstruct the propagation of Originium dust with any effectiveness.
We didn't have any other way.
Maybe you want to tell me how the sick could decide in their own final moments to leave the monastery, and go out into the wastes alone to wait for death to take them?
We can only bury this pain out of sight, and cover our ears, and turn not to look... We could never save anybody, and there's no one who could save us.
But even this facade of serenity is waiting to be shattered in the end.
And here we are. You've come.
Our mission here is to maintain order.
Of course. It wasn't any of you that destroyed our life here, after all. It simply and naturally came to the way it is now, as a matter of course.
And no matter how carefully I tend to the flowers I plant, when it's time for them to wilt, they will transform in invisible measures until they're beyond recognition. It's all the same.
You have been affected by Arturia.
You mean by Seniora Arturia's music?
Perhaps. In them, I often hear recollections of these things.
But she's never been a part of our lives. I know, she simply takes our distress, our suffering, as ornaments for her leisure...
And so, how could she ever go far enough to disturb our wills, to make these choices for us?
...Dangerous articles detected.
You have concealed a large quantity of flammables beneath this sacrarium—no—this entire monastery.
Yes.
It was a... possible alternative.
Whether you came or not, the tranquil life of this monastery was never going to last.
Have you seen what hunger and fear of wandering the barrens can drive people to, Senior Federico?
I have.
Before things reach the point of no redemption... I will end it all.
Clément Dubois, you are a suspect in the destruction of the Sanctilaminium Ambrosii, and are involved in multiple cases that concern or have concerned the rights of Lateran citizens.
You have been confirmed an executorial target by the Notarial Hall. In accordance with regulation, I will immediately proceed to apprehend you.
Federico never gives more warning than needed. The gun in his hands simply serves the place of speech.
Clément has not collapsed.
The wasting man clutches at his arm, where all feeling is lost, and gives a bitter smile.
Destruction of lighter confirmed.
Why... won't you kill me?
Why go to the bother of aiming for the lighter?
Your actions have not directly led to the deaths of any Lateran citizens. The law would not sentence you to death.
The law... So many years I've lived here, hearkening to His Excellency's guidance, following your law.
I feel its bind in ordinary times... Yet in chaos, in the time we need it most, it disappears without a trace.
There was some point where the music of Seniora Arturia became all I could even hear.
Senior Federico, is it the law of Laterano that wants to save me... or is it you, yourself?

lines.st: We should have something to look forward to in life.
It's a funny thing to say out loud. Isn't it granted, after all?
If we don't expect the slightest thing out of life, then why are we endowed with life, and born unto this world, anyway? It can't be just to suffer, can it?
You see, children don't think about such strange questions.
Oh, childhood, how carefree were your days. Only when we've left it behind, can we then appreciate its worth.
For our adolescence, and for our prime, the same thing goes. Filled with worries moment to moment—yet in time, you look back, and wonder to yourself what those little worries even meant.
Back then, we had strength we could never use up. We always wanted to do something or other—we always believed life would get better.
And so we were earnest. And so we strove to live.
How strange. Wasn't everything meant to get better?
It's hard to say at what point it happened, but it was like I'd lost the vigor to long for the future.
Narrower grew the spaces that could accommodate me. Thinner grew the air that could give me breath. Now my life was heavy, and I could see the end of it, and it... had hope no more.
As if a stone rolling down a mountain.
And you can see, and I can see, it's not ever going to stop. It'll just fall, and fall, and fall.
Until it's shattered apart.

lines.x: My flowers... they're all gone.
Do not step forward.
This part of the building has suffered repeated structural damage. It has a high degree of instability...
Urgh—!
Clément, come back.
No, Senior Federico... I need... to look one more time.
You previously stated that the flower you are looking for is not here.
I did, I just wanted to check... all the flowers are dead. And not one... not a single one... had the luck to be saved.
Ahh... ha... hah...
I am unable to analyze your behavior.
I am unable...
I planted these flowers myself... to stand for friendship and hope.
But they stopped caring about those things long ago. Nobody follows the flowering seasons anymore. They forgot the one thing above all they shouldn't forget.
Isn't it ridiculous? How some could suspect Raimund of the fire, just because he's a Sarkaz?
After living beside each other for so long. The harder the times, the closer we should come together... so His Excellency says. I want like nothing else to believe him... and by all rights, I should.
But the truth is placed before me, and I can't turn my head away.
Why do barriers never cease to be? Is mankind doomed never to understand one another?
The tiniest chaos is enough to shatter the order. The moment confusion envelops, people begin to hurt one another...
Enough... I've put up with it for long enough.
Clément staggers his way before the holy statue, scorched in the fire.
No second lucky flower is there. Only an old wooden tray, holding a few slices of driest bread.
A staff of life somewhat strange in scent. Into his mouth it goes, and he chews.
It is a faint brininess, with a flavor as astringent as the tides.
*cough*, *cough* *cough*—Disgusting...
Clément, put down that object.
Put it down? No...
*cough* *cough*...
Last night, I was intending to go to confession, but ended up overhearing the Abbot's plan...
It made me think—surely, at least Gerald and his people didn't need to take part? They were about to leave anyway, weren't they?
But why... did it all turn out this way?
I'll eat of this until it's gone.
You are not doing this to prolong your own life. Why are you still attempting to change?
I just want an answer.
I want to know... if there's any being in this forsaken world that can save us.
Federico knows—he must stop the man before him, right now.
But in fact, he stands there, rooted in place.
Just a few steps outside, Arturia begins to bow her cello, strings cutting through quietus's curtain. The sentiments within, Federico does not grasp, and never tries to grasp.
But for the very first time, the executor cannot help his own actions.
He begins to lose his understanding of himself.

lines.st: Why?
Why did he not act the very moment he saw the other party?
Why, despite this feeble man consuming a thing that turns people to monsters, has nothing happened at all?

lines.x: The song rises, key wrested up by one tone.
Between notes, the silence that disperses seems a mocking joke.
*cough*, *cough* *cough*...
Ah... is this—blood?
So what—this is it? What's the—*cough* *cough*—what's the point—without...?
Respiratory rate quickening. It is possible that the blunt force trauma has caused bleeding in your lower respiratory tract or lungs. If steps are not taken, it may lead to suffocation.
You require prompt medical treatment. Until then, I will perform first aid on you.
No—*cough* *cough*—I...
Clément covers his mouth with his hands, and tries to stifle the sound of his coughing as hard as he can.
But still the blood spills forth from his palms, with a discomforting viscosity, dripping down along the creases in his fingers.
He staggers backwards, and tumbles to the ground before the statue.
The graven image he burned almost seems now to accept him once more, murderer bereft of strength that he is. Its broken arms fall to either side, as if in gentle embrace.
Clément feels a wave of peculiar warmth.
Hah... hahah...
Look at me, aren't I entirely alright...?
So even this turned out to be false. The Abbot was made a fool of. There's no God out there...
*cough*...

What you ate originates from some kind of oceanic detritus. It is exceedingly probable to have induced mutation on the physiological level, but it has never had anything to do with the God you speak of.
Hah... ridiculous...
You are in terrible condition.
If you continue to worsen at your present rate, you will enter shock, and the risk of death will be enormous.
Really? That's just... not bad...
If you wish to leave any last will or testament, I am able to serve as your executor nominate.
Will...?
I don't have anything to leave...
Lateran... Our Paradise is a lie. Your faith is hollow.
Any friendship, faith, hope, future... it's all gone. No one can really be saved...
No one... no one can truly find redemption...
Is this a sort of choice? Is this some kind of happy ending?
The executor stands there, and moves no further forward.
Beyond the touch of anything or anyone, all his tribulations gradually fade away.
The weary gardener finally closes his eyes.
Still the lingering sound echoes, still I can feel it... A shake from the strings to my fingertips in the final moment, a last note so sharp it could tear skin.
Yet at the outset, that melody was calm as a babbling brook.
Someone otherwise so ordinary used his own life to prove one thing—that there is no such thing as a Savior. His unyielding mind triumphed over his own changing flesh.
Such beautiful music... This is the spirit of Mankind, tenacious and free, of a timbre those lamentable sea creatures could never know.
This song will bring his story to all.
They will understand his feelings, exalt his determination, and sing praise of his courage.
Clément Dubois will be remembered forevermore.
—Wouldn't you say so, Federico?
I was expecting you'd stay there a while longer. After all, you seemed to care greatly about the man who died... You even thought his affairs were higher priority than capturing me.
Federico, have you learned compassion? Or are you still as you were, unable to discern the emotions others feel, estranged from the crowd?
Fugitive Arturia, your Originium Arts have once again induced the death of another. I cannot ignore the greatest threat within my firing range.
Per the laws of Laterano, your sentence will be extended again.
It was his will to seek death. Even had I not performed, he would still take this path.
Time after time, I simply shed light on the scars across these poor people's hearts, and what do bystanders do but turn and look away?
Are you stung by his experiences, just as others are?
Federico, you still can't make sense of uncontrollable urges even now, can you? Just as the abbot persists along his path of thorns...
...Just as I cannot help but want to perform the emotions of others.
I certainly cannot make sense of them.
Whether I do or not, it has no impact on my implementation of the law.
But I proceeded to the sacrarium so that Clément would earn his rightful treatment.
He was not worthy of death.
—Oh?
You wanted to save a man who intended to destroy himself.
Haven't you realized? That's not a notion logic would fit at all.
Clément said that once a flaw, imperfection, or wound manifests in something seemingly perfect, it is broken beyond repair.
...I will test the accuracy of this statement.
You have become just a little more amusing, Federico... but only that little bit. Perhaps, come next we meet, that's when I'll be able to play your mind.
Sadly, it's time I wrap this trip up.
Excuse me, might you be Frau Arturia?
I've been waiting for you.
My apologies—we were the ones who extended an invitation to you as our guest, and yet I was late to arrive. Finding this place was an ordeal.
Erm, have I perhaps come at a bad time?
Federico!
Man oh man, I look at this scene and my brain is a pretzel.
Federico, how did it happen that you faced off with Arturia?
Good morning, Herren of Lateran.
Good... morning, gnädige Frau.
(Federico, her coat of arms is from one of the Kurfürsten, the Principes Electores!)
You can't make a direct move against Arturia right now. Laterano-Leithanien ties would be at risk...
...I refuse your suggestion.
Are you done talking it over?
Actually. How about, Federico, I give you one chance?
If you want to point your gun at me, then this is where you want to aim...
The black-haired woman clasps the executor's tense gunhand in her own.
She presses the muzzle against her own forehead.
Just pull the trigger, and your bullet will pierce my skull. And from then on, I would never bother you again... Federico, do you want to do that?
Why don't you give it a try?
I am required to arrest fugitives. If the situation does not necessitate, then shooting to kill is generally not advised.
But your argument is extremely rational.
What are you—?! Listen to me, this could be an INTERNATIONAL INCIDENT!
Hold on, Oren, I don't think things are that serious...
No, she will be fine. You do not understand this woman.
Heheh. I'm only playing a little game.
Frau Arturia, is this some kind of applied Arts?
Even so, what you just did was far too dangerous.
Thank you for your concern. I'll be cautious next time, should there be one.
Federico, think of this as a clue from me to you.
Have you ever felt like there's a performance that never ends, but you just can't hear it?
That until its grandest, most wonderful passages, everyone is deaf to it?
Well, then. The music is over, and it's time I went on my way again.
Herrschaften of Laterano, auf Wiedersehen.
If you'll excuse us.
You're actually just gonna stand there and let them go?
I thought it'd take at least Richele and me at the same time to hold you back.
You are extremely noisy.
What? Hey, Federico, where're you going?
He's headed for the area by the clock tower. If I remember right, that's where... the abbey's cemetery is?
Alright, no more gawking, all's well that ends well. Plus, we've still got things to do...
Senior Federico?
Seniora Arturia...?
They're gone...
It's okay, let's go look somewhere else!
Eep! Why's there a man lying down here?
Shh, he's asleep. We shouldn't wake him up...
Oh!
I know who this man is. He grows really pretty flowers! Same kind as Mamma always brings home to put in the vase!
Mmm...
What are you doing, Sara? You know Mamma gave us that?
But—he might catch a cold sleeping here...
Mamma said good kids learn to be app... precive.
You mean appreciative.
Okay, we'll let him borrow it for a bit then!
Mmm. Once he wakes up, I hope he'll keep growing heaps and heaps of pretty flowers.
Yeah, I'm sure of it!
Hey, wing-man.
I'm talking to you! Hey, come back!
Assuming that 'wing-man' is an epithet for Sankta, there are multiple individuals who fit the name.
I'm talking to you. I know your name is Federico.
Very well.
You really know how to piss people off, don't you?

I don't have time for your crap.
That guy, Oren...
He said that Gerald, he... for us, for me and Fortuna...
Is it true? Is Gerald really—
Yes, Gerald is dead.
Now that you answered, I'm starting to wish you didn't.
I don't get it... how could it be...
...I'm sorry, but this is a fact.
Ha... what are you sorry about?
Bah...
Keep your distance, wing-man. Close your eyes. Don't look at me.
That thing on your head is way too bright. It hurts my eyes. I just need a second to adjust.
Very well.
Gerald gave you his knife. The choice was his. It's not my place to argue.
But I can't let you take it away.
You have to know what it means to take the weapon of a Sarkaz mercenary.
It means carrying on the legacy of his name.
Yes. That's why I'm going to take it back from you, along with Gerald's name, and his honor.
I challenge you.
You won't win.
I have to.
As long as I draw breath, I'll find you, and challenge you.
Don't you dare underestimate me.
I refuse your challenge.
H-How dare you?!
I'm challenging you to an honorable—
Gerald was a mere hunter. There is no legacy to inherit in this knife.
What do you mean?
Exactly what I said.
If you want a knife that inherited the name of a Sarkaz mercenary, I have never been in possession of one. You're wasting your time challenging me.
If you want the knife of Gerald the Hunter, then yes, I am currently in possession of it.
Why you...
Do you want it?
...Burdenbeast dung.
This was Gerald's treasure. He wouldn't never let me take it just like that.
You keep it safe.
You just wait... I'll lead my people to find a new path, just like Gerald did.
I'll come back for it when I've earned it.
Very well.
I will keep it safe, until then.
Raimund...
Stop gawking. I think he can take it.
What about you? You said you couldn't hold a gun anymore. Are you... praying with it?
Yes...
I have a feeling it will answer me again.
Can I still use it, Serpilia?
Why not? It may be a little old, but you can trust my handiwork.
I used to study antique guns, you know.
Lately, though, I'm finding new things a little more interesting.
Do new things scare you at all?
There's no point being scared. One wants to learn more, in spite of the fear.
Besides...
Somebody once told me that techniques exist to be used.
I didn't understand at the time. But now...
Now?
I understand a little. I'm a craftsman, and I'm responsible for the weapons I craft.
Well, set that aside for a bit. What about you?
Are you ready to leave with the Sarkaz?
H-How did you know what I was going to say?!
No empathy needed to guess your thoughts.
It's not such a big deal when you put it that way, is it?
You wouldn't come looking for me at this hour with that solemn look on your face, if you were planning to come back to Laterano.
...Sorry.
I promised to come quietly and accept the punishment I deserved... but now I'm trying to run away.
S-So...
So, will I let you go?
Take a guess.
Er...
Your whole deal here is kind of a headache. You'll have to wear a hood all the time, if you live in Laterano.
I-I see, I guess that won't work...
Not that it'll be much better anywhere else.
You'll need to hide your horns, your appearance, and your gun.
And I'll still be your warden. You'll have to keep in touch with me, no matter where you go, as long as you live.
You won't cause me any trouble, will you?
—Er, um.
Wait, you're letting me go? W-Won't you get into trouble for that?
Worst case scenario, I quit my job at the Curia.
That was a joke.
It's true that I'll have some reports to write when I get back. I'll think about whether to get started on a resignation letter after that.
Anyway, you owe me more than a pizzelle now.
Th-Thank you... thank you so much.
Are we... still kin?
Does it matter? You're not getting off the hook, kin or not.
I want those pizzella next time I see you.
The convoy is almost here. The abbey can start moving once it's refueled.
Well, this was a tricky case, even though the location wasn't that far from Laterano. I think I should borrow a bed at the abbey, get some rest.
I didn't see you do anything particularly tiring, Richele.
Weren't you pretending that you didn't know anything?
Or should I thank you for not being lazy enough to dump all the evidence linked to the Church of the Deep into the sewers?
Don't be so uptight, Oren.
I'm sure Federico remembers the Sarkaz mercenary's dossier better than I do. And you and Lemuen are definitely more familiar with Church of the Deep cases than I am.
Plus, don't you think we're starting to get along a little bit, after you gave up empathy?
Why are you bringing that up?
No reason. Just, sometimes it's better to know less about a guy, like how you have to close one eye when you shoot.
Just to make it clear, though, I took Lemuen's instructions about confirming whether the abbey had anything to do with the Church of the Deep seriously.
At the end of the day, I suppose we can all sleep better at night.
You're lucky... or should I say, we're lucky.
The monastery gets the supplies it needs to return to Laterano. The disruption to the inhabitants was minimal.
The fallen girl has gone with the Sarkaz. Laterano will be able to find her, and the Sarkaz, if need be.
Laterano's image has not been materially affected.
Things don't turn out so well every day.
I prayed for a smooth trip to Laterano, safe from bandit attacks.
We should be fine once we get to Laterano.
I hope so! The emissaries from Laterano don't look like they've ever been hungry, and their clothes are brand new. I envy them.
How do they plant their crops? Or do they give everyone a gun to hunt with?
Who knows? I can't imagine. I can't imagine what I would do with my time, if I didn't have to spend all my time fixing houses and making tools.
I'm trembling, and I can't tell whether it's from excitement or fear.
Let's pray. Our faith will protect us.
Well, we did our best, didn't we?
As for whether things will turn out well in the end...
Do you know where Mamma went?
A child? I thought everyone was gathered up here.
Mamma's not here... but she came back last night!
Yeah, Mamma brought us a gift last night. We lent it to a sleeping man...
Sleeping?
He was sleeping really deep! He must have been having a nice dream.
An abbey built upon a mobile plate, moving again after being stranded for a decade, almost becoming one with the land...
An impressive sight, the convergence of destruction and rebirth.
Yes...
You seem troubled. Do tell, if you feel unwell.
Your transformation is yet incomplete. The path ahead will be laborious.
I'm fine.
They...
Will the Laterans treat those children well?
I believe so. The sitting pope is known for moderation and benevolence. They will likely be taken in by foster parents and grow up in Laterano.
Traveling with the fallen angel, and with the eyes of Sankta from the Curia on them, it is unlikely that your kin will face any retribution.
I'm glad to hear that...
Only one thing, Bishop Aulus.
They're not my kin anymore.
If that is what you think, then that is what it will be.
The stranded Paradise is no more. It is time for us to set out.
Once again, we welcome you, Hyman.
What are your plans?
Sinners cannot be choosers.
You may have had the intention to cause harm, but at the end of the day, you didn't do any real damage.
Personally, I see no problem, but Velliv might be a little stricter where Iberia is concerned...
Ah, it doesn't seem like you're very happy with that answer.
I apologize for the discourtesy I showed, Reverend Nuncio.
I sense your sincerity. I accept your apology.
But I also sense that it's not what you really want to say.
It's not an easy thing, judging oneself...
It is the truth that I fell to despair, and knelt before the malice in my heart.
What is more deserving of punishment: Harm caused out of benevolence, or malice not put into action?
To me, it's the former.
An idea could be fleeting, or it could take root silently, biding its time.
How could you know that this is simply the result of a single moment of weakness?
What would this place be like, if I chose another path?
I can't say that I've purged the caprices from my mind... I can't even say that I still believe in our laws...
But you still feel it, don't you?
Yes, I do...
Every waking moment, I prayed that the people would be given their due, prayed that this little corner of Paradise would not slip through my fingers.
But I've waited for so long...
Too long. So long that I began to doubt that the very existence of faith was simply a self-imposed conceit.
How does it feel?
Despair, fear, sorrow... what else? The feeling of...
Incredulity.
How do we doubt something that exists in one's own mind?
It means you've begun to doubt yourself... with terrible consequences, if one is unable to dispel the doubt.
If even faith could be false, then how much of Stefano is real?
I wanted to understand. I tried to understand. But I overestimated myself.
We could never truly understand another. 'Understanding' remains precious, fleeting, even with the incredible power of empathy.
But having experienced so much, I have began to think that there are many emotions that can be communicated from one to another, and understood by most, even without the empathy.
At least, I can say that I have began to understand the thoughts of a certain individual, not because I sensed something, but because I have experienced it myself...
I offer this to you, Bishop Stefano.
It's...
The notebook of a cleric who spent some time here. I thought you might find it... interesting.
Perhaps, just like you, he had many questions, and many troubles.
You asked me about my plans.
I plan to return to Laterano. But not with the others.
You mean you want to travel from here to Laterano by yourself?
At your age...?
Please don't stop me. I must know. I must... rediscover my faith.
If there comes a day when I atone for my sins, if there is truly such a thing as miracles, perhaps I will find my Laterano then.
In that case...
I wish you the best of luck.
I hope you find your answer, Father Stefano.
We will meet again in Paradise.
The old man says no more.
Age has taken its toll on him, as have the ordeals of last night. The lines of age fill every gap on his face.
Before Lemuen's eyes, Stefano Torregrossa walks out the door, his back slightly hunched.
Executor Federico, reporting in.
As previously reported, the two nuncios are confirmed alive and well. They are expected to return to their posts in Laterano soon.
Plans are in place for Sanctilaminium Ambrosii to resume movement, setting a course for Laterano's outskirts. It is expected to arrive at its destination before Spring next year.
A request for supplies has been made to the Curia to ensure reliable mobility, and to meet the everyday needs of the inhabitants.
Forty-one of the one hundred fifty-three inhabitants of the monastery will depart the abbey. Serpilia will track and report on their subsequent movements.
The Sarkaz you mentioned?
I must admit my surprise, even though I had a hunch...
Whether it was the state of the abbey, the movements of the Church of the Deep, or Arturia...
You've done well, Federico.
I failed to meet your expectations.
Our intervention destabilized the situation at the abbey.
There were... three casualties.
You have doubts, my child.
...Yes.
I can rectify a wrong if I see it. I know many ways of stopping a sinner.
But at no point during this mission was there a clear course of action.
Why was the result suboptimal, if no error was committed?
Are our laws suitable for all circumstances? If not, on what do I base my actions?
That is where my doubts lie.
I don't have an answer for you, Federico, because this is a question that troubles me as well.
But I'm happy that you're beginning to ask these questions.
I don't understand.
There is no need to be anxious. You're beginning to ask questions that you've never considered before. Is that not a big step forward?
It's like pulling the cork from a bottle of fine wine. The hardest part is over. All that is left is to pour out what you want.
I'm going out to visit a friend, one that you're not unfamiliar with. I'll take your question to him.
And share with you the answer he offers.
The towering statue is silent. The rays of the morning sun pour in. The executor ends the call and looks up.
The aging Sankta's silhouette begins to fade into the morning sun as he slowly walks away.
As she sees him off, Lemuen gets a feeling.
The feeling that she has not seen the last of the old man.
As he said himself—
Perhaps he will find the Laterano that he is looking for.
